pages.title: How to read an MSFH inspection report before you choose a project
pages.description: Use the report to confirm the recommendation, the cost notes, and the 1802 summary before you choose a project or compare quotes.
pages.quickAnswer: Start with the recommended improvements section, the estimated costs, and the 1802 summary. That tells you which program route is open before you jump to contractor shopping.
pages.workflowMeaning: This page owns the program-side handoff between inspection and grant. It is not approval by itself, and it is not the moment to sign the first contractor quote you receive.
coverageNotes: The report may list recommended improvements, cost estimates, and the 1802 summary.
The grant and inspection remain separate applications.
Only recommended or later observed improvements should move forward.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: If your home is attached or treated like a townhouse, check scope before assuming roof-related work is eligible.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume the most expensive project is the best first project.
Do not assume every opening or every roof element automatically qualifies.
Do not assume the report alone means reimbursement is locked in.

primaryCta.label: Confirm the report route before you shop quotes

secondaryCta.label: Check which work is actually reimbursable

relatedLinks.label: Choose the first project after the report
Use the quote checklist after this report step

pages.title: Which MSFH project should go first?
pages.description: Choose the first project by grant alignment, recommendation strength, and what the quote path actually needs next.
pages.quickAnswer: The best first project is usually the recommendation that is both clearly eligible and most likely to unblock the grant path without unnecessary extra scope.
pages.workflowMeaning: Project choice should stay tied to what the report actually recommended, not to whichever contractor pitch feels largest or fastest.
coverageNotes: Opening protection can be the clearest first path when the report makes the opening scope explicit.
Roof-related work may be right first only when the eligible recommendation truly depends on it.
Grant alignment matters more than generic home-improvement value.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached-home cases should pause before assuming roof replacement is the first answer because program scope can narrow sharply.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume roof replacement is covered just because the roof is old.
Do not assume every recommendation can be bundled into one reimbursable project.
Do not assume your attached home follows the same scope rules as a detached home.

primaryCta.label: Compare likely project paths before you commit

secondaryCta.label: Pressure-test opening protection as the first project

relatedLinks.label: How the inspection report feeds project choice
When roof replacement becomes the first project

pages.title: How to vet an MSFH contractor quote before you sign
pages.description: Use the quote stage to verify contractor fit, permit responsibility, and scope before you confirm or sign anything.
pages.quickAnswer: Match the contractor type to the recommendation first, then verify license, permit responsibility, and whether the quoted scope lines up with the report before you sign.
pages.workflowMeaning: This page owns the program-side quote handoff. The homeowner still owns contractor choice and project management, but the route keeps you from turning a recommendation into a denial risk.
coverageNotes: The program no longer provides an authorized contractor list.
The contractor should fit the actual mitigation recommendation, not just the broad category.
Permit handling and final closeout still matter.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: If the home is attached, verify that the quoted scope does not drift past opening-protection-only funding before you commit.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume the first quote is safe just because the contractor mentions the program.
Do not assume a general roofing quote is the same thing as an SWR-aligned quote path.
Do not assume the program will fix documentation gaps later.

primaryCta.label: Confirm the quote route for your recommendation

relatedLinks.label: What the program may pay for

pages.title: What the program may pay for under current MSFH rules
pages.description: Stay close to the authorized improvement list and the actual recommendation before you budget around reimbursement.
pages.quickAnswer: The program may pay only for recommended or later observed eligible improvements, and roof-related reimbursement usually needs to stay tied to that eligible scope.
pages.workflowMeaning: This page exists to separate grant-aligned work from broad home-improvement wish lists before the quote stage gets expensive.
coverageNotes: Authorized categories remain opening protection, roof-to-wall, roof deck attachment, and SWR-related work.
Matching grants and low-income grants are different paths under the current rules.
Work started before approval may be denied.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached homes may see the answer narrow to opening protection even when a detached-home owner would read the same report differently.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume a general reroof is automatically reimbursable.
Do not assume the grant covers every construction line item attached to an eligible project.
Do not assume the same scope rule applies to detached and attached homes.

primaryCta.label: See what the safest next step looks like

secondaryCta.label: Check when roof replacement is actually allowed

relatedLinks.label: Choose the first project after eligibility is clear

pages.title: MSFH report says no recommended improvements
pages.description: If the report shows no recommended improvements, the next step is usually clarification, not immediate contractor shopping.
pages.quickAnswer: When the report shows no recommended improvements, the grant path usually does not move forward. Verify the result before spending on quotes.
pages.workflowMeaning: This support-state route explains the dead end clearly and sends you back to the main decision wedge only if your facts change.
coverageNotes: No recommended improvements usually means there is nothing eligible to submit right now.
This is not the moment to buy generic mitigation work and hope it qualifies later.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached-home rules do not change the basic no-recommendation outcome.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume you can self-select a project and recover reimbursement later.
Do not assume a contractor can convert a no-recommendation report into a grant path by itself.

secondaryCta.label: Go back and confirm the report result

pages.title: MSFH Group 5 status and timing block
pages.description: Use this route when Group 5 is blocking prioritization or access timing and you need the next action spelled out.
pages.quickAnswer: Group 5 is a status-layer issue, not a project-selection answer. The next step is usually to find out whether you can proceed at all before collecting quotes or planning a grant-backed job.
pages.workflowMeaning: This page keeps support-state confusion away from the main wedge around recommendations and contractor choice.
coverageNotes: Portal group timing can block or delay the practical next step.
This status does not create new eligible improvements by itself.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached-home scope rules still matter later, but Group 5 is primarily an access-timing issue.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume a delayed group status means contractor work should start anyway.
Do not assume support-state frustration changes reimbursement rules.

secondaryCta.label: Go back to project choice only if timing clears

relatedLinks.label: Portal statuses that change the next move

pages.title: MSFH RFI request blocking your file
pages.description: Use this route when an RFI is holding the file and you need the corrective path before you sign or start work.
pages.quickAnswer: An RFI usually means the file needs more information before it can move. Resolve the missing item before treating the project as approved or reimbursable.
pages.workflowMeaning: This page stays corrective rather than expansive so support-state confusion does not turn into premature contractor action.
coverageNotes: RFI responses should be handled before you treat the grant path as secure.
Documentation gaps can slow or block the project path.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached-home scope does not override missing information requests.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume an RFI is a minor clerical note with no project consequence.
Do not assume you should sign or start work while the request is unresolved.

secondaryCta.label: Re-check eligible work before you reply

relatedLinks.label: Contractor quote route after the file clears

pages.title: MSFH portal statuses and what they change
pages.description: Use this route when status language changes the practical next step for a homeowner already moving through the program.
pages.quickAnswer: Status pages are useful only if they change what you should do next. If the status does not unlock a recommendation or a quote path yet, treat it as operational context rather than a sign to start work.
pages.workflowMeaning: This support route sends the user back to the main decision wedge once the status question is clear enough.
coverageNotes: Portal language can explain timing or missing-file issues.
Status visibility does not replace grant-aligned project selection.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Scope rules still apply after the status question is resolved.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume any portal status is the same thing as reimbursement certainty.
Do not assume a status label tells you which contractor type to hire.

secondaryCta.label: Go back to the report path once status is clear

relatedLinks.label: What to do after the report

pages.title: MSFH final inspection and draw request after work is done
pages.description: Use this route when work is complete and you need the closeout sequence for final inspection and draw payment.
pages.quickAnswer: Final inspection and draw-request work happen after project execution, so the right move is usually to verify permit closeout and file readiness rather than reopen project selection.
pages.workflowMeaning: This route stays corrective. It does not replace the earlier decision work around recommendation interpretation, quote fit, or grant-aligned scope.
coverageNotes: Permit closeout still matters before final inspection.
Operational timing after project completion can affect reimbursement flow.
pages.attachedHomeCaveat: Attached-home rules matter earlier in the workflow; this page is mostly about completion-stage operations.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume completion-stage paperwork fixes a bad earlier scope decision.
Do not assume every contractor handles the closeout details for you.

secondaryCta.label: Go back to quote prep if closeout is still unclear
